### SEO Description

```
🚨 WARNING: If you've taken Cipro, Levaquin, or Avelox — your tendons may still be at risk up to 6 MONTHS after your last dose. This is the danger window most doctors don't explain.

Dr. Victor Kane reveals the hidden mechanism behind fluoroquinolone tendon rupture — and it starts with magnesium chelation at the atomic level. If you're over 60, on corticosteroids, or have reduced kidney function, your risk is 3-5x higher.

In this video, you'll learn:
• The 3 antibiotics with FDA black box warnings for tendon rupture
• WHY the danger window extends 6 months beyond your last dose
• The magnesium chelation mechanism destroying collagen fibers
• 3 warning signs your tendons are weakening BEFORE rupture
• The exact 3 questions to ask your doctor about antibiotic alternatives
• Why corticosteroids + fluoroquinolones = 3-5x risk multiplier

📚 Sources: FDA Black Box Warning (2008-2018), CDC Antibiotic Prescribing Guidelines, ~3.2 ruptures per 10,000 FQ prescriptions in over-60s

⏱️ Timestamps:
0:00 — The 6-Month Danger Window Nobody Talks About
1:15 — The 3 Antibiotics With Black Box Warnings
2:45 — Magnesium Chelation: How Antibiotics Destroy Tendons
4:30 — Who's at Highest Risk (Age 60+, Steroids, Kidney Function)
6:00 — 3 Warning Signs Before Rupture
7:30 — The Doctor Conversation Script (3 Questions)
9:00 — What To Do If You've Already Taken These Antibiotics

⚠️ IMPORTANT: Never stop prescribed antibiotics without consulting your doctor. This video is for awareness and informed conversation with your healthcare provider.

🔔 Subscribe for weekly health insights for adults over 60.
📩 Share this with anyone who's taken Cipro, Levaquin, or Avelox.

#AntibioticTendonRupture #CiproWarning #Fluoroquinolone #Over60Health #DrVictorKane
```

## VIDEO 2: VID-028 — Nocturnal Hypoglycemia

**Working Title:** "Why You Wake Up Sweaty and Confused After 60 — The 3 AM Blood Sugar Crash Your Doctor Isn't Testing"
**Upload Target:** Thursday, June 19, 2026 at 2:00 PM EST
**Idea ID:** I128 | **Score:** 22/20

| 2 | The 3 AM Blood Sugar Crash: Why Beta-Blockers Mask Hypoglycemia After 60 | 68 | P067: "The [Time] [Condition]: Why [Drug] Masks [Problem] After [Age]" |
| 3 | Your Meds May Be Dropping Your Blood Sugar at 3 AM — The Danger After 60 | 69 | P025: "Your [Container/Habit] May Be [Harming Action] Your [Health Aspect] After [Age]" |

**Recommended:** Title #1 — uses the "specific time + symptom" hook (P024, 19 points) combined with the "doctor misses" authority gap (P016/P020 family). "Sweaty at 3 AM" is a visceral, relatable symptom that drives clicks from personal experience. Exactly 70 chars — at the limit but not over.

### SEO Description

```
Do you wake up sweaty, confused, or with a racing heart around 3 AM? It might not be anxiety — it could be a blood sugar crash your doctor isn't testing for.

Dr. Victor Kane explains nocturnal hypoglycemia: the dangerous blood sugar drop that happens while you sleep, especially if you're over 60 and taking a combination of diabetes medication, beta-blockers, and ACE inhibitors.

In this video, you'll learn:
• The 3-drug interaction stack that silently drops blood sugar at night
• WHY beta-blockers mask the warning signs of low blood sugar (the "Muted Alarm" effect)
• The HbA1c blind spot — why your "normal" diabetes test misses nighttime crashes
• The 3 AM Protocol: a zero-cost self-detection method you can start tonight
• When to call your doctor immediately vs. when to monitor
• The exact conversation framework to discuss medication timing with your physician
• ADA 2024's new recommendation on sulfonylureas for older adults

📚 Sources: ADA Standards of Care 2024, Diabetes Care 2023 (88% of nocturnal episodes missed on standard monitoring), ACE inhibitor insulin sensitivity research

⏱️ Timestamps:
0:00 — Miriam's Mystery: Sweaty, Confused, and No One Could Explain Why
1:30 — What Happens to Blood Sugar While You Sleep
3:00 — The 3-Drug Stack: Sulfonylurea + Beta-Blocker + ACE Inhibitor
5:00 — The Muted Alarm: Why Beta-Blockers Hide Hypoglycemia
6:30 — The HbA1c Blind Spot (The Math That Explains It)
8:00 — The 3 AM Protocol: Zero-Cost Self-Detection
9:30 — When to Call Your Doctor (Emergency Red Flags)
11:00 — The Doctor Conversation Framework
12:30 — Miriam's Outcome: What Happened After the Diagnosis

⚠️ IMPORTANT: Never adjust diabetes medications without consulting your doctor. If you suspect nocturnal hypoglycemia, contact your healthcare provider. Stopping beta-blockers abruptly is dangerous.

🔔 Subscribe for weekly health insights for adults over 60.
📩 Share this with anyone who takes diabetes medication or beta-blockers.

#NocturnalHypoglycemia #BloodSugarCrash #BetaBlockerWarning #Over60Health #DrVictorKane
```
